1.synthesis: Paul turns to the collection for the saints in Jerusalem. He gives the Corinthians the same instruction he gave the Galatian churches: each person is to set aside a sum of money on the first day of every week, proportional to what God has given, so that no collection has to be made when Paul arrives (1 Corinthians 16:1–2). When he comes, he will send whomever the Corinthians approve—with letters—to carry their gift to Jerusalem; if it seems advisable that he should go too, they will travel with him (1 Corinthians 16:3–4). Paul plans to come through Macedonia and perhaps stay with the Corinthians for a while, or even spend the winter, so that they may help him on his way wherever he is going (1 Corinthians 16:5–6). He does not want to see them merely in passing; he hopes to spend some time with them if the Lord permits (1 Corinthians 16:7). He will stay at Ephesus until Pentecost, for a wide and effective door has opened there, though there are many adversaries (1 Corinthians 16:8–9). If Timothy comes, they should see that he has nothing to fear; he is doing the Lord's work just as Paul is, so let no one despise him—send him on his way in peace (1 Corinthians 16:10–11). Regarding Apollos: Paul urged him strongly to come with the brothers, but he was quite unwilling to come now; he will come when he has opportunity (1 Corinthians 16:12).
1.historical_context: The Jerusalem collection is one of the best-documented features of Paul's apostolic ministry, described also in Romans 15:25–28, 2 Corinthians 8–9, and Galatians 2:10. Paul understood it as both practical relief for a poor Jewish-Christian community and a sacramental act of Gentile-Jewish unity—the Gentile churches' material gift to Jerusalem was a tangible declaration that the dividing wall had been broken down. The instruction to set aside funds on the first day of the week (v.2) is the earliest reference to Sunday as the Christian assembly day. The Galatian churches received the same instruction, suggesting a coordinated collection across Paul's mission field. Paul's travel notes reveal his pastoral instinct: he will not visit Corinth briefly but wants a substantial stay—the relationship is too important for a passing call. The 'wide and effective door' at Ephesus (v.9) matches the Acts 19 account of the Ephesian ministry, where for two years all in Asia heard the word. The mention of many adversaries is consistent with the Demetrius riot (Acts 19:23–41).
1.literary_structure: The travel notes and collection instructions (vv.1–12) serve as the letter's administrative close, transitioning from the doctrinal and ethical content of chapters 1–15. The collection instruction is given briefly because the Corinthians already have the Galatian precedent to follow; the more elaborate treatment comes in 2 Corinthians 8–9. The travel plans (Paul via Macedonia, Timothy potentially, Apollos unwilling to come now) sketch the Corinthian church's relational network: Paul is the absent father, Timothy the trusted representative, Apollos the independent colleague who will not be pressured. The candid admission that Apollos 'was quite unwilling to come now' (v.12) is notable precisely because the Corinthians had formed factions around Apollos (1:12)—Paul does not manage this by forcing Apollos to visit but by honoring his freedom.
1.theological_application: The collection instruction embeds a theology of proportional, pre-planned generosity: 'each of you is to put something aside and store it up, whatever he may prosper' (v.2). This is neither the coercion of a tithe command nor the impulsiveness of spontaneous giving but the deliberate, regular allocation of resources proportional to one's means—discipline in the service of love. The weekly setting-aside on the Lord's Day connects the community's worship to its material solidarity: the day of resurrection is also the day of preparing for the needs of others. The community of the risen Lord is a community of mutual material care that spans geographic and ethnic boundaries. Paul's travel contingency—'if the Lord permits' (v.7)—models Christian planning: specific intentions held with open hands, subject to the Lord's direction, without the paralysis of false humility or the presumption of certainty. The church at its best plans concretely but holds its plans before God rather than treating them as fixed.
1.christology: The collection for Jerusalem is grounded in the grace of Christ: 'you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich' (2 Corinthians 8:9)—the theological premise Paul makes explicit in 2 Corinthians but assumes here. The Lord's Day (v.2) is the day of Christ's resurrection; to set aside material provision for the poor on this day is to enact the resurrection community's character—life shared rather than hoarded. Timothy doing 'the work of the Lord' (v.10) is shorthand for the apostolic mission in Jesus' name; the Lord whose work is being done is the risen Christ, and all apostolic labor is participation in his continuing mission.

13.synthesis: Paul closes with a rapid series of imperatives: 'Be watchful, stand firm in the faith, act like men, be strong. Let all that you do be done in love' (1 Corinthians 16:13–14). He urges the Corinthians to be subject to people like the household of Stephanas—the firstfruits of Achaia, who have devoted themselves to serving the saints—and to every fellow worker and laborer (1 Corinthians 16:15–16). He rejoices at the coming of Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus, who have made up for what was lacking from the Corinthians themselves; they have refreshed Paul's spirit as well as the Corinthians'. Give recognition to such people (1 Corinthians 16:17–18).
13.historical_context: The five imperatives in verse 13 are drawn from military and athletic vocabulary—a standard form of ancient exhortation. 'Be watchful' (grēgoreite) is the sentinel's call; 'stand firm' is the soldier's posture in battle; 'act like men' (andrizesthe) means 'show the courage of a man' rather than a gender prescription. These imperatives recapitulate the letter's major concerns in compressed form: the Corinthians have been passive before factional pressures (chs.1–4), morally passive before sexual sin (ch.5–6), and theologically passive before false teaching (ch.15). The household of Stephanas (v.15) was mentioned in 1:16 as those Paul baptized; they are the Achaian church's founding family and have traveled to Ephesus to meet Paul, likely bringing the Corinthian letter to which 1 Corinthians is the reply (7:1: 'Now concerning the matters about which you wrote'). Fortunatus and Achaicus are otherwise unknown; their names suggest freedmen or slaves of a household.
13.literary_structure: The compressed imperatives of vv.13–14 function as the letter's benedictory summary: the entire letter could be summarized as 'stand firm in the faith, act courageously, let all be done in love.' These five commands gather up the letter's major imperatives—doctrinal steadfastness (chs.1–4), moral courage (chs.5–6), and love as the supreme norm (ch.13)—into a single compressed closing charge. The Stephanas commendation (vv.15–18) is personal and specific: Paul names the actual individuals who have served both him and the community, models the kind of recognition he asks the Corinthians to extend, and closes the loop on the letter's origin (Stephanas's party brought news that generated the letter).
13.theological_application: 'Let all that you do be done in love' (v.14) is the compressed form of chapter 13, placed as the capstone of the five closing imperatives. The juxtaposition is deliberate: watchfulness, firmness, courage, and strength without love become the very disorders Paul has been diagnosing throughout the letter—partisan confidence, moral rigidity, doctrinal pride. Love is not softness but the shaping virtue that gives all the other virtues their proper character. The call to 'be subject to' (hupotassesthe) the household of Stephanas and fellow workers (v.16) is not hierarchical command-and-obey but the voluntary submission the Corinthians have been unwilling to practice throughout the letter—recognizing actual service as the ground of authority rather than charismatic gift, rhetorical skill, or social status. Those who have 'devoted themselves to serving the saints' deserve the community's recognition precisely because their authority is grounded in love-in-action rather than status.
13.christology: The five imperatives in v.13 culminate in 'let all that you do be done in love'—and throughout the letter, love has been defined by the pattern of Christ's self-giving, who 'though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor' (2 Corinthians 8:9) and who gave himself up in the Lord's Supper pattern that Paul received from the Lord himself (1 Corinthians 11:23–25). The courage Paul calls for is not self-generated fortitude but the settled assurance that comes from knowing 'your labor in the Lord is not in vain' (1 Corinthians 15:58)—grounded in the resurrection of the one who is Lord. Standing firm in the faith is standing firm in the gospel of the crucified and risen Christ that Paul preached at Corinth's founding (15:1–4).

19.synthesis: The churches of Asia send greetings. Aquila and Prisca, together with the church in their house, send warm greetings in the Lord (1 Corinthians 16:19). All the brothers send greetings. 'Greet one another with a holy kiss' (1 Corinthians 16:20). Paul writes a greeting with his own hand (1 Corinthians 16:21). Then follows one of the most arresting sentences in the letter: 'If anyone has no love for the Lord, let him be accursed. Our Lord, come!' (1 Corinthians 16:22). The closing benediction: 'The grace of the Lord Jesus be with you. My love be with you all in Christ Jesus. Amen' (1 Corinthians 16:23–24).
19.historical_context: Aquila and Prisca (Priscilla) are among Paul's most significant co-workers—Jewish Christians expelled from Rome under Claudius, tent-makers like Paul, founders of house churches in Corinth (Acts 18:2–3), Ephesus (here), and later Rome (Romans 16:3–5). Their house church at Ephesus (v.19) is one of several attested house churches in Paul's correspondence. The holy kiss is attested across Paul's letters (Romans 16:16; 2 Corinthians 13:12; 1 Thessalonians 5:26) and becomes the liturgical kiss of peace in early Christian worship. Paul's autograph (v.21) authenticates the letter against forgeries—compare Galatians 6:11 and 2 Thessalonians 3:17. The Aramaic Maranatha ('Our Lord, come!') is preserved untranslated in a letter written to a Greek-speaking congregation—evidence that it was a liturgical formula already so well-established that it needed no translation, likely used at the Lord's Supper (cf. Didache 10:6).
19.literary_structure: The closing moves through several registers: community greetings (Asia, Aquila and Prisca), liturgical instruction (holy kiss), apostolic authentication (Paul's autograph), a stark eschatological warning (anathema for those without love for the Lord), the Aramaic prayer (Maranatha), and the double benediction (grace of Jesus, Paul's personal love in Christ Jesus). The anathema-Maranatha sequence (v.22) is the letter's sharpest moment: the curse and the prayer for the Lord's coming stand in juxtaposition, suggesting a eucharistic context where judgment and advent hope are simultaneous realities. The final benediction—'my love be with you all in Christ Jesus'—enacts what the entire letter has demanded: Paul's own love for the fractious Corinthian community, offered in Christ, the ground of all genuine community.
19.theological_application: 'If anyone has no love for the Lord, let him be accursed' (v.22) is the starkest statement in the letter and perhaps Paul's most direct invocation of eschatological judgment. It is immediately followed by 'Our Lord, come!'—the posture of those who do love the Lord and therefore welcome his return. Together they articulate the church's dual orientation: love for the present Lord who will come as judge, and the eager longing for his coming that only love generates. The community without love for the Lord has no ground to stand on at his coming; the community shaped by love for him prays for what it desires—his arrival. The closing words—'my love be with you all in Christ Jesus'—are Paul's final answer to the letter's central problem: a community fractured by competing loves (for Paul, Apollos, Cephas, status, spiritual gifts) is called back to the one love that creates genuine unity, the love of and in Christ Jesus that Paul himself embodies in writing this demanding, pastoral, profound letter.
19.christology: The letter that opened with 'Christ crucified' (1 Corinthians 1:23) closes with the prayer 'Our Lord, come!' (Maranatha)—the crucified Lord is also the coming Lord. The risen Christ whose resurrection is the firstfruits of the general resurrection (15:20–23) is the one whose return is both the community's hope and the moment of final judgment. 'The grace of the Lord Jesus be with you' (v.23) names Jesus as Lord—the title the whole letter has been unpacking: the Lord of the Supper, the Lord whose body is the community, the Lord who gives spiritual gifts, the Lord whose cross is the community's wisdom, the Lord whose resurrection is the community's future. The letter's entire argument is captured in that final formula: what the Corinthians need is not Apollos's eloquence, Cephas's Jewish heritage, or Paul's founding authority—they need the grace of the Lord Jesus, and this is freely given to all who love him.
